title: More than Just Algebra
summary: Mrs. Eggleston discusses the importance of relationships in education and the challenge of helping students see algebra beyond the futures they already know.
intro: Canaseraga Central School serves fewer than 200 students PK-12. In this conversation, high school math teacher Mrs. Eggleston reflects on what it means to teach in a place where classes are small enough that teachers know not just how students learn, but who they are outside the classroom as well.

questions.q: What does teaching here look like day-to-day?
a: So I'm from Jasper and I went to Jasper-Troupsburg, so I was familiar myself, kind of with like small school, small district, even Canaseraga even smaller than J-T, so I was kind of even shocked at that when I applied and got the 6th grade job, and, I mean, you're the only sixth grade teacher. So something that immediately was apparent was you don't have even one other teacher at your grade level to kind of like keep pace with or bounce ideas off over, do a project together. Like, you're the whole grade level where like, now I'm the whole algebra and the whole algebra II.

So that is sometimes a blessing and a curse. Like, you're not really accountable to somebody else. which can be good, but also there are times when it's kind of like I'm doing Regents exam review right now and I wonder, is this the best strategy that I'm using? Like, what do other people do and I don't really have anybody to ask so that is a challenge.

Day to day, I would say the best, the best part about it and what I think makes small districts more successful is that you you cannot help but get to know your kids even more, probably than you ever want to about them. But like the current ninth grade was the last class that I had in 6th grade. So when I decided to move up to math, I had them again in 7th again in 8th. And I will have half of them next year for the two-year track and I'll get the other half back the following year for algebra 2. So, I mean, that's going on five and six years in a row with the same kids.

So, in that particular group of kids, it's like, I just know them. Like, I can look at a lesson and instantly say, you know, this student is going fly through this. I'm going to have to make sure they have something else to do at the end. This student's going to struggle on two, five, and 7A. Like, seriously down to the kid down to the bush. Like, you can anticipate where their mind's going to go, what they're going to write, the mistake they're going to make, how to fix it. Like, it's, and that's, it's crazy to me. Like, even growing up in JT, would we have, you know, like a class size of 20. Like, I have a class size of five a lot of the time. So you really can know their strengths, their weaknesses, you understand their lives outside of school, and how that affects their achievement in school.

questions.q: How much does that personal connection affect students academically, especially students who may not see themselves as strong math students?
a: A ton. I even, I've always said that from sixth grade. Like, I know in college, you spend a lot of time with writing lesson plans and all the logistics of things, but you don't get a lot of time spent on things like building relationships, connections, classroom management, things like that, because there is often this trap of teachers who come in and they're new and they want to be liked and they want to form relationships, but so the management piece kind of comes at the expense of that. So then you have classes that get kind of wild and crazy because the teacher just wants to be popular versus setting boundaries. Then you have teachers who I think the boundaries are a bit harsh, they're strict, and then that kind of prevents the relationship from forming, and the cases that just are kind of like, oh, this person's a jerk, I'm not going to do work for them.

I think you have to have a middle ground, but if you can establish it, I mean, I have kids in here that you just,I guess I always think of it as like, I have to earn their respect. So once you have it, they will work much harder for you. Like, they will. Even on days when I'm out, I mean, the kids just know how the room is. They know me as well as I know them, they know me, so they know my expectations. They know if there's a sub in here and somebody, one class leaves the room a mess like they know where to put things and then I'm going to want to come in and the room be clean and then I would be upset if it wasn't.

But I think they work harder in class for sure when they know you know, I guess if they can see the effort that's going into the lesson getting the obligation, if they respect you, they'll respect that you worked on that and so then they'll work for you. Like they'll know Mrs. E is going to be so disappointed if I did bad on this because she knows I can do better. And so they'll do that much more work where you also kind of hear snippets of their conversations, you'll hear them kind of talk about other classes and like, you know, I was absent for three days, I'm going to do all this teacher's work first, and I don't care about this teacher's work kind of thing, but their attitudes about their teachers have an impact. Kids work better for teachers that they like, for sure.

questions.q: For students who ask, "When am I ever going to use this?", what do you do to make math feel more relevant?
a: I think as the years go on, the fact that you care about them, like, they know it, and they just kind of becomes more familiar, so it's less present in their mind. Like, the older they get, and the higher the stakes get with the exams and things, the more they're kind of like, well, this this stupid, why do I have to do it? Why do I have to pass this test? So there are times when like I'll adjust my curriculum to put more relevant situations in. You know, like math definitely has a stigma for, like, the word problems are crazy unrealistic and unrelatable. So I will adjust those and like make them about the kids themselves, like give them a question, put their name in it, things like that to keep them engaged.

I think one area that is lacking when it comes to rural education is making that connection. So for a kid who kind of has their mind made up, it's like, well, my dad's a welder and he makes good money, so I'm gonna be a welder when I graduate and I don't need this. Like, I think bringing in more just, like, education about careers and even careers outside of your immediate town where you live would really help them to see that they would be capable of that. Like, I just feel like the kids, a lot of the kids that I teach aren't thinking of, like, what happens after high school. It's not in their brain. It's either I'm a college kid or I'm a workforce kid. There's no, like, well, let me actually see what's out there. Like, what are jobs there? Like, I just feel like their view of that is so narrow and so localized and so it is often really hard to get them to see where a lot of like, upper level algebra for sure, like where is that going to be relevant in my life? Because they just don't have a concept of where that information could actually be useful.

So a lot of the way that I approach it, a lot of the times it's kind of like, well, it doesn't matter what you do with your life. At certain times you're going to run into challenges, like challenging tasks where you have to think them through, you have to problem solve, you have to do something you don't want to do. And for you right now as a 14 year old, algebra might be one of those things and you have to still give it your best effort and think it through and solve the problem. Like, if you think you know exactly where your life's going to go and what your job's going to be, like, you might have to pass a certification exam, Work with someone you don't like or there's always going to be a challenge that you have to take through. So I try to approach it that way, but I think if they had more of a concept of just careers in general outside of him that would help. Sometimes I see that when these kids talk about certain careers, it's not that they're super passionate about it or they love it, it's more of like, this is what my dad does so this is what I'll do. So I wish that there was more exposure to just see their options.

title: The Advantage of Small Classes
summary: Mrs. Eggleston discusses how small classes can change the way students learn, interact, and push each other.
intro: Canaseraga Central School serves fewer than 200 students PK-12. In this conversation, high school math teacher Mrs. Eggleston reflects on how very small classes shape learning, accountability, and the way students work together.

questions.q: Earlier, you mentioned that smaller schools can be more successful. What do you mean by that?
a: Well, every year we look at our test grades and compare them to the greater BOCES region. And I just think it always comes out that like, I think it's Canaseraga and Bradford is another super small district where the scores are always just kind of soaring compared to a lot of, you know, like bigger schools like Bath or Horseheads or Hornell, things like that. And I think part of that makes sense of like, at bigger schools you're going to just have a wider variety of kids. That's harder to meet the needs there. But you also have a lot more resources. So for me in algebra, one, like, those Regents scores are only mine compared to Hornell, which might have like three different teachers who contributed to those scores, and that's a lot of more knowledge and resources that they can devote and, you know, collaborate versus just me here. And then, of course, our numbers are skewed because where they might have hundreds of kids taking the same test, I've got 11. It can be good or bad, because if two kids don't do well, then my grades are just not going to look great.

But it just seems consistently that the small schools outperform the larger schools. And I think that's because you just have such a smaller chance of falling through the cracks. Like, if I had a class of 24 in here, one or two kids missing school here and there is kind of like a, okay, that's normal. I have a class of five and my one kid isn't here for three days, like, it's very apparent. And also, I can easily track that kid down and get them caught up one on one. So, and the teachers here are really good about working together on that. Like, there's not many of us, especially in the high school, so communicating with, you know, this one kid is currently absent. Everyone's kind of fighting for his or her time when we get them caught up. Like we are really good at working out systems for that.

And so I think just that, just physically being present is noticeable. And then, of course, if I have a class and five kids, like we talked about before, I know them very well, but I can easily pinpoint where they're struggling and give them more time and attention than I could if I had a much bigger group. So I just think the advantage is a lot more one-on-one time with your teacher, just kind of like centralized help specific to you.

questions.q: What role do very small classes, like a class of five, play in academics? Do you see students becoming closer, helping each other, or pushing each other more in that setting?
a: I do. It's interesting to look at the dynamics between like the five kids that I have in here at a time versus when the global teacher has the whole class together. Like she sees, I have two students that always want to work together, always help each other. And it's kind of like a friendly competition-based thing, but they'll help each other too. But in global, they don't even speak to each other. So it's kind of like I definitely see it in here. They form these kind of like miniature friendships and bonds that might not exist outside of the algebra classroom, but in here they will help each other and work together. And I think that is cool.

And it's kind of, I kind of like the competition part of it. Like, they're not competing for anything. It's really more like with each other. But they'll push each other, which I appreciate because I kind of feel like that's also kind of a downside with the small school like this is usually, you know, you'll have one or two kids who are way top of their class and everyone else is like miles behind and some of those kids kind of just feel like, I'm just not a smart kid. I'm just not toppling my class and they kind of give up. But when I have them in here in a really small class, they will really compete with each other and it's a good environment.

title: The Realities of Teaching
summary: Mrs. Eggleston reflects on what first drew her to teaching and what she wishes more people understood about the responsibility, difficulty, and purpose behind the profession.
intro: Canaseraga Central School serves fewer than 200 students PK-12. In this conversation, high school math teacher Mrs. Eggleston reflects on her path into the profession and what she wishes more people understood about the work teachers do.

questions.q: Could you talk about what first brought you into teaching, and then what led you specifically into teaching math?
a: Teaching-wise I think just, I kind of just feel like I always wanted to since I was younger. I don't know. My mom ran a daycare growing up, so it's always just kind of around little kids and taking care of them. And my mom does a lot of educational aspects in the daycare and just growing up, I am just always the person who just loved school. So it kind of made sense.

When I originally went to college, I didn't major in education right off the bat. It was math and science. Just those were my preferences. And then I ended up at Cortland for my education. And even then, it really just evolves as you go because I think people have a certain picture of what it means to be a teacher. And then once you get into it, it's actually such a broad area like you can do so much. So I went for elementary education originally. And when this was my very first job, like outside of subbing and student teaching and all that. When I first student taught, I thought for sure second grade was my preference and that was my first placement and then I moved to fourth grade and I looked at that a lot more and then I got hired here, it was sixth grade and I was kind of like terrified middle schoolers might not be my thing, but then I loved that.

And I loved it the whole time that I was in sixth grade, but what really, I mean, math has just always been my preference. The way that I ended up in this position was that we had a teacher who left on maternity leave and didn't come back. and then there was a long-term sub in her position. And when she left, nobody applied. And I had kind of said to my superintendent at the time, like, wow, I can't believe nobody wants to just teach like high school math all day. That's the best. And he was like, oh, wow, don't you do that. Now I'm doing it. And it is the best. And I just teach all the algebras. So my first year, I started, I did the math 7, math 8, and then the two-year algebra track. And now you shuffle things around and I just have all the algebras. It's two and all the sections and I love it.

questions.q: What do you wish more people understand about the profession?
a: I think teaching in general is a challenging job. I think I just meet a lot of people who are headed into that field and on one hand, I love to see that because there is definitely a shortage going on right now. And on the other hand, every time I hear it I'm just kind of like, are you sure? I really do think that previously what attracted people to the profession was the idea of like, good retirement system and you have summers off and, you know, a lot of breaks during the school year and things. And I think some of those benefits have kind of diminished over the years. Like, if you're in tier six, the retirement is not not what it was. That is challenging.

And for a lot of people, like you don't really have the summer off. Whether you're developing a new curriculum for next year where you're on a committee that comes in and meets during the summer where you teach summer school or you're sick of education, but you still need the money so you do like landscaping, so many teachers still work over the summer. And I just think it, I don't know, I think people kind of view it as like glorified babysitting and don't think that people understand, like, I have a master's degree in order to be here. So it is a challenging job and even people who go from just like subbing day-to-day to like a long term, like a maternity leave where you might be here for three months, like, don't really understand the concept of planning that far ahead, just everything that goes into it a day-to-day responsibility of the teacher.

So I guess something I just wish more people knew was that it's a very challenging job. Like, the days off are not because it's not a valuable profession. It's because you really need it.And so I always encourage people when I hear that they're going into education, you really, really, really have to want to do it. Like, you really have to enjoy being around kids. You really have to want to help them. Like, you see just a lot of teachers, I feel like who don't enjoy it, or, you know, who are annoyed by the kids. And of course there are days that are annoying for sure. But I think if you're someone who like, by your third year, you're kind of like, ugh, I hate these kids, I can't wait for the end of the year, that's not going to bring you joy in your life so that is something I would just say to anybody, no matter where you're going to teach.

But then also just related back to the rural piece. Like, I love working here. I've only worked here. I hope I only ever do work here. I love these kids. I love the class sizes. But I think there is a massive shift going on in education towards regional high schools. Guidelines are changing away from Regents exams so that, you know, a kid might not have to choose a Regents path to get to graduation. It's called Portrait of a Graduate. So it's kind of like a kid might choose more of a fine arts path over math and science, but somehow we have to make them equally as rigorous. And, I mean, we just can't offer that here. Like logistically, there's no way. I'm already over my contractual teaching obligations, so there's just no physical way for me to teach more.

And I don't know how our music department would be able to find something as rigorous as an Algebra II Regents. So there's a lot to be worked out, but I think the thinking behind it is that smaller schools, at least at the high school level, will be phased out and will kind of funnel into these big regional schools. And I am very interested to see, I guess, what the results of that are because like I just said, I love the small classes. I think that the small class sizes really lead to a higher success rate and I think it feels better for the kids. I think it's what's better for teachers. So, I don't know. I'm definitely anxious about that shift for our state.

title: A Look at STEM Electives
summary: Mrs. Gault recounts her inclusion of forensics and biomimicry in the biology curriculum, and what these electives reveal about student engagement when science is connected to the real world.
intro: Wethersfield High School serves a small rural community in central Illinois. In this conversation, science teacher Mrs. Gault reflects on building out the district's science offerings over 18 years—from freshman bio to forensics and biomimicry—and what those electives reveal about student engagement when science feels more visible and connected to real life.

questions.q: Could you introduce yourself and tell us a little more about what you teach here?
a: My name is Jennifer. I teach freshman biology, which is Bio 1, Bio 2, which is a semester of forensic science and a semester of biomimicry, and then earth science.

Last year, I taught chemistry as well. So it depends on the years. About five years ago, I was also teaching environmental science and a semester of botany. So in a smaller school, it depends on numbers and how many classes of each we have or need, and so you might be filling in other subjects.

When I started here 18 years ago, there were one and a half science teachers, and then a couple of years after that, it went to two science teachers, and now we're at one and a quarter science teachers. So it depends on, again, the department's needs. So the other science teacher is teaching math as well. He is teaching physics and chemistry this past year, and then he's teaching math as well.

questions.q: I haven't heard of forensics and biomimicry being taught in small schools. Were those courses offered because students were interested in them, or because they were areas you wanted to teach?
a: Well, the forensics is something that I was interested in when I first came. And again, just being handed the book and said, here, it's what you want. Freshman bio was a lot easier for me to figure out because you're like, okay, I need cells, I need genetics, and I need ecology, you know, evolution, so you're like, okay, that's good.

And then you mentally go, they've already had a year of this, what am I going to do with Bio 2? And you can go more into depth into human systems. It's been done to death, but anatomy is what the other teacher was teaching. And so I didn't want to do that since they had that other option.

So since I had taken a course at Harper Community College on forensics, I figured, okay, here's a semester and a course that we can put together. And so I've been adding to it for the 18 years on and off based on what I had known then and then what I found out since then, as well.

And the biomimicry is, several years ago, I went back for a master's, and it was in biomimicry. And so then when we needed to add it in a semester course, I added that in. Didn't have a lot of takers for the class, because it's a class that needs a good decent-sized class because a lot of it is collaborative work and talking and bouncing ideas off each other once you have some of that initial information. So with a smaller population, it really didn't go over well, especially at the beginning.

questions.q: What is the engagement like in forensics as it's an elective?
a: So here, the state requires two science classes to be able to graduate. So some of them are simply going, this is my second science, I don't really want to take chemistry or physics. So, yes, they're engaged, but not necessarily as much as somebody who really is interested in it.
You then have the other group who's really interested in that field or into, you know, those TV shows or CSI. So you have a mix of the two in that class.

questions.q: What tends to be the difference between the two groups?
a: A decent size of our population have family members who know someone who is a corrections officer or a police officer, or even the National Guard has a station. So you have some of those, and so I think you have a little bit more of, okay, this is hands-on, I can see where it would apply, versus in Bio 1 when you're dealing with genetics and cells, like, okay, I can't even see what you're talking about and I have to take your word for it.

title: The Inclusion of Agriculture in STEM
summary: Inspired by a conversation with Mr. Blunier, this piece explores agriculture as a field deeply rooted in STEM—and how long-standing divides between agriculture and STEM education have kept the two apart, even when the work on the ground suggests they belong together.
credit: Inspired by a conversation with Mr. Blunier

title: When Friends Lead the Way
summary: After talking with a group of students deeply involved in their town's FFA, I saw how much friends shape who joins extracurricular STEM—when a few students get involved, others often follow, simply because people they know are already there.
credit: Inspired by conversations with local FFA members

title: A New Generation
summary: After speaking with Mrs. Canfield, an elementary teacher at Wood River who runs a summer STEM camp, this piece explores how she gets kids excited and involved in STEM long before high school. Each year, students choose the camp theme—from birds of prey to Taylor Swift—and she uses those topics to show STEM in a light that feels fun, relatable, and worth showing up for outside the classroom.
credit: Inspired by a conversation with Mrs. Canfield

title: When STEM Isn't Cool
summary: After speaking with a student at Wood River High, this piece looks at the opposite of STEM camp—where it can feel cool to dislike STEM and school, where almost no one else in her grade shares her love of learning, and where her motivation to go to college has come largely from her parents at home. Most students do not have that kind of support from parents, teachers, or friends, and this conversation explores how hard it is to stay involved and excited without it.
credit: Inspired by a conversation with a Wood River High student
